content:
A Lightweight Diabetes Screening Model for Low-Resource Rural Settings: A Proof of Concept
Tesfaye H. Kifle (Independent researcher and Scholar of AMII)
Calgary, Alberta, Canada
Date: June 30, 2026
Keywords: Diabetes screening, machine learning, Random Forest, low-resource settings, community health workers, non-invasive features, BRFSS, developing countries, rural health, proof of concept, Vertex AI, BMI, hypertension, public health informatics
Abstract
Diabetes mellitus is a growing public health challenge disproportionately affecting low- and middle-income countries, where rural communities often lack access to laboratory-based diagnostic infrastructure. This study presents a proof-of-concept machine learning model for non-invasive diabetes screening using four readily obtainable features: Body Mass Index (BMI), Age, High Blood Pressure (HighBP), and General Health status (GenHlth). A Random Forest classifier was trained on the CDC Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) 2015 dataset, comprising 70,692 balanced records. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 73%, precision of 71%, and a recall of 77% for diabetic cases. The model was successfully deployed to Google Cloud Vertex AI, demonstrating production-level deployability. This work demonstrates that a simple, low-cost screening tool based on non-invasive inputs can be developed and deployed for use by community health workers in under-resourced rural settings.
1. Introduction
Diabetes mellitus represents one of the fastest-growing chronic disease burdens globally. According to the International Diabetes Federation (IDF), approximately 537 million adults were living with diabetes in 2021, with projections reaching 783 million by 2045. The majority of this growth is concentrated in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), where healthcare systems are often ill-equipped to manage the scale of the epidemic (IDF, 2021).
In rural communities across developing regions, the barriers to diabetes diagnosis are significant. Laboratory-based diagnostic tools — such as fasting blood glucose tests, HbA1c measurements, and oral glucose tolerance tests — require equipment, trained personnel, and supply chains that are frequently unavailable at the community level. As a result, a substantial proportion of diabetes cases in these settings remain undiagnosed until complications such as neuropathy, nephropathy, or cardiovascular disease emerge (WHO, 2016).
Machine learning offers a promising avenue for bridging this diagnostic gap. By identifying patterns in readily available, non-invasive patient data, ML models can serve as preliminary screening tools that flag high-risk individuals for further clinical evaluation. This approach does not replace clinical diagnosis but rather extends the reach of early detection to settings where conventional diagnostics are not feasible.
This study presents a proof-of-concept Random Forest classifier trained on a large, population-representative dataset to screen for diabetes risk using only four non-invasive features collectible by community health workers without laboratory equipment.
2. Data
The dataset used in this study is the CDC Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) 2015 Diabetes Health Indicators Dataset, publicly available through Kaggle (Teboul, 2021). The BRFSS is an annual telephone survey conducted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention collecting health-related behavioral data from over 400,000 Americans (CDC, 2015). The cleaned version of this dataset contains 253,680 survey responses with 22 features including demographics, health behaviors, and chronic condition indicators.
To address class imbalance between diabetic and non-diabetic respondents, the balanced subset of the dataset was used, containing 70,692 records with equal representation of diabetic (1) and non-diabetic (0) cases — 35,346 each. This balancing ensures the model learns both classes equally, which is critical for a health screening tool where missing a diabetic case carries greater consequence than a false positive.
3. Feature Selection
Feature selection was guided by two principles: clinical relevance to diabetes risk and practical collectability in low-resource settings without laboratory equipment.
The following four features were selected:
BMI (Body Mass Index): A well-established risk factor for Type 2 diabetes, measurable with a basic scale and height measurement. Obesity and overweight are among the strongest modifiable risk factors for diabetes onset (Hu et al., 2001).
Age: Diabetes risk increases significantly with age, particularly after 45 years. Age requires no equipment to collect.
HighBP (High Blood Pressure): Hypertension and diabetes are closely linked conditions, frequently co-occurring in the same individuals. Blood pressure measurement requires only a basic cuff, widely available even in rural clinics (Sowers et al., 2001).
GenHlth (General Health): A self-rated health measure on a scale of 1 (Excellent) to 5 (Poor). This single survey question requires no equipment and has been shown to correlate with chronic disease burden including diabetes (Idler & Benyamini, 1997).
The following features were explicitly excluded:
Physical Activity: Rural farming communities are inherently physically active by occupation, making this feature a poor discriminator in the target population.
High Cholesterol: Requires laboratory blood testing, contradicting the non-invasive design principle.
Smoker: Not sufficiently prevalent in many rural communities in developing regions to serve as a meaningful population-level predictor.
4. Methods
4.1 Model Selection
A Random Forest classifier was selected for this study. Random Forest is an ensemble learning method that constructs multiple decision trees during training and outputs the majority class prediction. Compared to a single decision tree, Random Forest reduces overfitting and improves generalization, making it well-suited for health classification tasks on large datasets (Breiman, 2001).
4.2 Train-Test Split
The dataset was split into 80% training (56,553 records) and 20% testing (14,139 records) using a fixed random state of 42 for reproducibility. The 80/20 split was chosen to maximize the volume of training data while retaining a sufficiently large held-out test set for reliable evaluation.
4.3 Evaluation Metrics
Three primary metrics were used to evaluate model performance:
Accuracy: Overall proportion of correct predictions across both classes.
Precision: Of those flagged as diabetic, the proportion that truly are diabetic.
Recall: Of all actual diabetics in the test set, the proportion correctly flagged by the model. Recall was considered the most critical metric for this application, as missing a diabetic case in a rural screening context carries greater health consequence than a false positive that prompts unnecessary but harmless follow-up.
The scikit-learn library was used for model training and evaluation (Pedregosa et al., 2011).
5. Results
The Random Forest model achieved the following performance on the held-out test set of 14,139 records:
Accuracy: 73%
Precision: 71%
Recall: 77%
F1-Score: 74%

FIGURE 1 — Classification Report and Confusion MatrixThe confusion matrix revealed the following breakdown:
True Negative: 4,859
False Positive: 2,231
False Negative: 1,620
True Positive: 5,429
Of 7,049 actual diabetic cases in the test set, the model correctly identified 5,429 — representing a recall of 77%. This means that for every 100 diabetic individuals screened, the model flags 77 for further clinical evaluation using only four non-invasive inputs.
The model was successfully registered in Google Cloud Vertex AI Model Registry and deployed to a live prediction endpoint. A test instance with BMI=32, Age category=7, HighBP=1, and GenHlth=3 returned a prediction of 1 (diabetic), confirming end-to-end production deployability.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

FIGURE 2 — Vertex AI Endpoint Test showing prediction=16. Discussion
6.1 Strengths
This study demonstrates that a clinically meaningful diabetes screening signal can be extracted from just four non-invasive features, all of which are collectible by community health workers without laboratory infrastructure. The use of the CDC BRFSS dataset — a large, population-representative survey covering both sexes and diverse demographics — lends credibility to the generalizability of the approach beyond a specific population.
The choice of Random Forest as the classification algorithm provides robustness against overfitting, which is particularly important when a model is intended for deployment in varied real-world contexts rather than controlled research settings.
The recall of 77% is particularly meaningful in the context of rural screening. In settings where diabetes often goes undetected until late-stage complications emerge, a tool that correctly flags three out of four diabetic individuals for follow-up represents a meaningful improvement over the status quo of no screening at all.
The successful deployment to Vertex AI demonstrates that this model is not merely a research artifact but a deployable digital health tool, capable of integration into mobile or web-based community health worker applications.
6.2 Limitations
Several limitations must be acknowledged. First, the training data originates from a United States population surveyed via telephone, which may not fully reflect the demographic, dietary, and lifestyle characteristics of rural populations in low- and middle-income countries. Validation on locally collected data from target communities would be a necessary next step before any real-world deployment.
Second, the model achieves 73% overall accuracy, which, while reasonable for a proof-of-concept screening tool with only four features, falls short of the performance thresholds typically required for clinical decision support tools. The model is explicitly intended as a first-line screening aid to prioritize individuals for further clinical evaluation, not as a standalone diagnostic instrument.
Third, the GenHlth feature relies on self-reported general health perception, which may be subject to cultural variation in how individuals assess and articulate their health status across different populations.
Finally, the Age feature in the BRFSS dataset is encoded as a categorical variable representing age brackets rather than exact age in years, which introduces some loss of granularity compared to direct age recording.
6.3 Generalizability to Low-Resource Settings
Despite these limitations, the core methodology of this study is designed with generalizability in mind. The four selected features — BMI, blood pressure status, age, and self-rated general health — are universally obtainable across diverse geographic and economic contexts. A community health worker equipped with a basic scale, a blood pressure cuff, and a structured questionnaire could collect all four inputs in a matter of minutes per patient.
In rural communities across sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and other regions where laboratory-based diabetes screening is not routinely available, this type of lightweight model could serve as a first-line triage tool, directing limited clinical resources toward the individuals most likely to benefit from further evaluation and early intervention.
7. Conclusion
This study presents a proof-of-concept machine learning model for non-invasive diabetes screening designed for deployment in low-resource rural settings. Using a Random Forest classifier trained on the CDC BRFSS 2015 dataset, the model achieves 73% accuracy and 77% recall for diabetic cases using only four features — BMI, Age, High Blood Pressure, and General Health status — none of which require laboratory equipment to obtain.
The model was successfully deployed to Google Cloud Vertex AI, demonstrating production-level deployability and t
